concepts.lesson: The Cartesian plane is a two-dimensional plane used to locate points using ordered pairs of numbers. It is formed by two perpendicular number lines called coordinate axes. The horizontal axis is called the x-axis and the vertical axis is called the y-axis. These axes intersect at a point called the origin. Every point on this plane is represented by an ordered pair (x, y), where x represents the horizontal distance from the y-axis and y represents the vertical distance from the x-axis. This system helps in describing exact positions of objects and is widely used in mathematics, science, mapping, and computer graphics.
concepts.real_example: The location of a point on graph paper represented as (3,4).
key_points: Two perpendicular number lines form the plane
Used to represent positions using coordinates

concepts.lesson: The coordinate axes are the two perpendicular lines that form the coordinate plane. The horizontal line is called the x-axis and the vertical line is called the y-axis. These two axes intersect at a point called the origin, represented by the coordinates (0,0). The origin acts as the reference point from which all distances on the graph are measured. Positive numbers are marked to the right of the origin on the x-axis and above the origin on the y-axis, while negative numbers are marked to the left and below respectively.
concepts.real_example: The intersection point of axes on graph paper is labelled as O(0,0).

concepts.lesson: Every point in the Cartesian plane is represented by an ordered pair (x, y). The first number, called the x-coordinate or abscissa, represents the distance of the point from the y-axis measured along the x-axis. The second number, called the y-coordinate or ordinate, represents the distance of the point from the x-axis measured along the y-axis. The order of the numbers is important because (x, y) and (y, x) represent different points unless both numbers are equal.
concepts.real_example: The point P(4,3) means the point is 4 units from the y-axis and 3 units from the x-axis.

concepts.lesson: The coordinate axes divide the Cartesian plane into four equal regions called quadrants. These quadrants are numbered I, II, III, and IV in the anticlockwise direction starting from the positive x-axis. Each quadrant has a distinct sign pattern for coordinates. In the first quadrant both x and y coordinates are positive. In the second quadrant x is negative and y is positive. In the third quadrant both x and y are negative. In the fourth quadrant x is positive and y is negative.
concepts.real_example: Point (2,3) lies in the first quadrant, while (-3,4) lies in the second quadrant.

concepts.lesson: Points that lie on the coordinate axes have special coordinate forms. Any point on the x-axis has a y-coordinate equal to zero because it has no vertical distance from the x-axis. Therefore, its coordinates are written as (x,0). Similarly, any point on the y-axis has an x-coordinate equal to zero because it has no horizontal distance from the y-axis. Hence, its coordinates are written as (0,y).
concepts.real_example: Point (5,0) lies on the x-axis and point (0,4) lies on the y-axis.

concepts.lesson: The origin is the point where the x-axis and y-axis intersect. It is represented by the coordinates (0,0). The origin acts as the starting point for measuring distances along both axes. All other points in the coordinate plane are located relative to this point. Understanding the origin is important because it helps students determine directions, quadrants, and distances when plotting points.
concepts.real_example: When plotting points on graph paper, the centre point where axes meet is the origin.

concepts.lesson: The sign of the coordinates of a point depends on the quadrant in which it lies. In the first quadrant both coordinates are positive (+,+). In the second quadrant the x-coordinate is negative and the y-coordinate is positive (-,+). In the third quadrant both coordinates are negative (-,-). In the fourth quadrant the x-coordinate is positive and the y-coordinate is negative (+,-). Recognizing these sign patterns helps students quickly identify the location of points.
concepts.real_example: Point (-4,-3) lies in the third quadrant because both coordinates are negative.

concepts.lesson: Coordinate geometry connects algebra and geometry by representing geometric figures using numerical coordinates. It allows mathematical relationships to be studied using equations and graphs. This system is widely used in physics, engineering, navigation, architecture, computer graphics, and data visualization. By converting geometric problems into algebraic expressions, coordinate geometry makes it easier to analyse shapes, distances, and positions.
concepts.real_example: GPS systems use coordinate systems to determine locations on Earth.
